metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: AC1's oracle ('a test asserting … the disabled path does not start a server') does not verify the second half of the AC's own requirement: that the refusal message 'names the flag'. The message-content assertion is stated in prose but absent from the oracle.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: AC1 — 'refuses to start with a message naming the flag. Oracle: a test asserting … the disabled path does not start a server'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the oracle: 'a test asserting no fastapi module is imported by `import rebar`, the disabled path does not start a server, AND the stderr/stdout message contains "[ui] enabled".'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge story E as a prerequisite (or specify a stub/placeholder read layer) so the live end-to-end AC is independently satisfiable within this story's scope.
findings.finding: The live end-to-end AC depends on the read layer from 'story E', which is a sibling story not yet delivered; this unacknowledged prerequisite means the AC's end-state-observable proof cannot be satisfied until story E ships.
findings.location: Scope — server.py ('one index route using the read layer from story E'); Acceptance Criteria — live end-to-end AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites / dependencies' section noting 'Story E (audit read layer) must be merged first, OR the index route uses a stub that returns a hardcoded list for this story only, replaced when story E lands.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the story-E dependency explicitly and specify the read-layer interface (module path + function signature) this plan's `server.py` will call, or provide a stub/protocol that this story implements directly so AC5 is self-contained.
findings.finding: AC5 requires the server's index route to list 'a seeded audited ticket' by calling 'the read layer from story E', but (a) no dependency edge on story E is declared and (b) the interface (module path, function signature) of that read layer is not specified in this plan. If story E is in-flight or its interface changes, AC5 is unverifiable in-session.
findings.location: AC5 ('lists a seeded audited ticket') and Scope → server.py ('one index route using the read layer from story E')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Depends on story E (read layer). Expected interface: `from rebar.audit.store import list_audited_tickets() -> list[str]`. If story E is not yet merged, `server.py` uses a thin stub (`return []`) and AC5 seeds data via that stub.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC (or explicit 'stub with NotImplementedError/helpful message') for the `show` dispatch arm, or remove `show` from the dispatch description if it is out of scope for this story.
findings.finding: The Scope section lists `_audit` as dispatching both `serve` and `show`, implying `show` is part of the dispatch wiring built in this story, but there is no acceptance criterion covering `show` and no description of what it implements — leaving it as an uncovered work item.
findings.location: Scope section: 'CLI: register `audit` as an INTERCEPT … `_audit` dispatches `serve`/`show`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to Scope: '`show` is registered as a stub that prints "not yet implemented" and exits 1; no AC required beyond non-crash.' Or remove `show` from the dispatch list and note it is added in the sibling story.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `show` in the Scope section (even if just 'stub that prints a not-yet-implemented message') so the executor knows exactly what to wire into the `_audit` dispatcher.
findings.finding: The `_audit` dispatcher is described as dispatching `serve`/`show`, but `show` has no scope bullets, no acceptance criterion, and no behavioral definition anywhere in the plan. An executor cannot implement the dispatch without guessing what `show` does.
findings.location: Scope → CLI section: '`_audit` dispatches `serve`/`show`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Scope bullet: '`show` subcommand — stub only in this story: print "not yet implemented" and exit 0; full rendering deferred to the sibling story.' Add a corresponding AC: 'Running `rebar audit show` exits 0 with a "not yet implemented" message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the sequencing contradiction: either clarify that story E (read layer) is a prerequisite and update the 'ships first' rationale, or rewrite AC5 to use a stub/seeded data source so this story genuinely ships independently.
findings.finding: AC5 requires 'the read layer from story E' to serve a real index listing audited tickets, yet the plan's Why/Approach sections position this story as shipping first ('the riskiest architectural piece … ships first as a thin vertical slice'). These two cross-section claims contradict each other: if this story ships first, story E's read layer cannot yet exist.
findings.location: AC5 vs. Why section ('ships first as a thin vertical slice') and Approach rationale ('Ship a thin vertical slice … so the risky packaging/gating/binding path is exercised end-to-end from the first story')
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC5 to use a hardcoded stub ticket list (no story-E dependency) so this story is truly self-contained, and note that wiring to the real read layer is story E's responsibility.

findings.finding: `src/rebar/config.py` re-exports every `_config_schema` class with an explicit `from rebar._config_schema import XConfig as XConfig` line. The plan says to re-export `UiConfig` from `config.py` but does not enumerate this as a concrete line-level edit, and the module has 12 explicit re-export lines already established — `UiConfig` must be added as a 13th. The plan's vague phrasing 'this module re-exports every `_config_schema` class' may lead an implementer to believe the re-export is automatic.
findings.location: Scope section / `src/rebar/config.py` — re-export `UiConfig`
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope: `src/rebar/config.py` — add the exact line `from rebar._config_schema import UiConfig as UiConfig` to the edit description, and remove the parenthetical '(this module re-exports every _config_schema class)' which is inaccurate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or fold into AC1's oracle) that `docs/config.md` documents the `[ui] enabled` key and `REBAR_UI_ENABLED` env var.
findings.finding: The Scope section names `docs/config.md` documentation of the `[ui]` key as a deliverable ('document the key in `docs/config.md`'), but no acceptance criterion verifies that this documentation was written or is correct.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/config.py — re-export UiConfig … document the key in docs/config.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1's oracle: 'and docs/config.md contains an entry for `[ui] enabled` and `REBAR_UI_ENABLED`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add WCAG 2.1 AA baseline done-definitions to the index page scope: semantic HTML landmarks, keyboard-navigable ticket links (WCAG 2.1.1), sufficient contrast (WCAG 1.4.3), and screen-reader-accessible list markup.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new user-facing HTML UI (an index page listing tickets, served in a browser) with no mention of WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility requirements — no keyboard navigation, screen-reader, contrast, or reduced-motion done-definitions are specified.
findings.location: What section ('serving a read-only INDEX page listing tickets') and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope under server.py: 'Index page uses semantic HTML (`<main>`, `<ul>/<li>`, `<a>`) satisfying WCAG 2.1 AA: keyboard-navigable links (2.1.1), 4.5:1 contrast (1.4.3), no color-only indicators (1.4.1).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a user-facing docs target (e.g., `docs/audit-ui.md` or a README section) covering `rebar audit serve` usage, the `--host` flag and its warning, `nava-rebar[ui]` installation, and the loopback-only no-auth rationale — and reference it from the Scope section alongside `docs/config.md`.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new CLI subcommand (`rebar audit serve`), a `--host` flag with a non-loopback warning, a new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra, and a loopback-only no-auth security posture — but only mentions updating `docs/config.md` for the config key. The subcommand usage, `--host` behavior, extra installation, and the justified no-auth posture (a novel architectural decision) have no identified documentation home.
findings.location: Scope section (`config.py` bullet: "document the key in `docs/config.md`"); AC bullets for `--host` warning and `nava-rebar[ui]` install message; 'Endpoint access contract' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/audit-ui.md` file (or equivalent) to the Scope list covering: (1) installation (`pip install nava-rebar[ui]`), (2) enabling via `[ui] enabled = true`, (3) `rebar audit serve [--host HOST] [--port PORT]`, (4) the non-loopback warning and its meaning, (5) the justified no-auth posture (loopback boundary, matching datasette/mlflow precedent). Reference it from the plan's Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `port: int = 8765` field to `UiConfig` (alongside `enabled`) and wire it through `_SECTIONS` coercers so users can set `[ui] port` in `rebar.toml` or via `REBAR_UI_PORT`, not just via `--port` CLI flag.
findings.finding: The default port 8765 is specified as a literal constant with no corresponding `[ui] port` config key, so users cannot persist a non-default port without always passing a CLI flag; this is a threshold expected to evolve (port conflicts, organizational convention).
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; What section — 'Default port 8765'
findings.suggested_fix: In `_config_schema.py`, extend `UiConfig` to `@dataclass class UiConfig: enabled: bool = False; port: int = 8765` and add `"port": lambda v, k: int(v)` to its coercer entry; pass `config.ui.port` as the default in the `serve` CLI handler.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a short ADR (or a `## Web UI security posture` section in `CLAUDE.md` / `docs/architecture.md`) recording the loopback-only = auth-boundary decision, the explicit out-of-scope for network exposure, and the precedent set by datasette/mlflow/git-instaweb.
findings.finding: The loopback-only, no-auth architectural posture is a novel security decision justified only in the ephemeral plan text; no ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update is called for, so future maintainers lack a durable rationale when someone asks 'why no auth?'.
findings.location: 'Endpoint access contract' section; Scope — `docs/config.md` mention

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In `server.py`'s `serve()` entry point, catch `OSError` (errno EADDRINUSE) around the uvicorn `run()` call and exit with an actionable message such as 'Port {port} is already in use — choose another with --port'.
findings.finding: No graceful error handling is specified for a port-already-in-use condition: if 8765 is bound, uvicorn raises `OSError: [Errno 98] Address already in use` which will surface as a raw traceback rather than a friendly actionable message.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; AC — end-to-end test AC
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap `uvicorn.run(...)` in a try/except OSError block; check `e.errno == errno.EADDRINUSE` and print a friendly message then `sys.exit(1)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a non-happy-path spec for port-already-in-use: catch `OSError` in `serve()` and exit with an actionable message (e.g., 'Port {port} is already in use; specify another with --port'); add a corresponding AC or note to the server.py scope.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the port-already-in-use error path for `rebar audit serve`, leaving users with an unhandled uvicorn exception instead of an actionable message.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — live end-to-end AC; Scope — server.py `serve(host, port)` entry
findings.suggested_fix: In `server.py`'s `serve()`, wrap `uvicorn.run(...)` in a try/except OSError and print 'Port {port} is already in use. Use --port to choose another.' then sys.exit(1).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the empty-state behavior of the index page (e.g., render 'No audited tickets yet' with a hint to run `rebar audit`) and add it to the server.py scope or ACs.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the index page 'lists tickets that have audit data' but does not define the empty-state UX when no tickets have audit data yet, leaving the page's behavior under a normal first-run condition unspecified.
findings.location: Scope — server.py index route; Acceptance Criteria — live end-to-end AC ('lists a seeded audited ticket')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an empty-state oracle to AC5: start the server with an empty/no-seeded audit store and assert the index returns HTTP 200 with a clearly defined empty-state indicator (e.g., an empty list or 'no audited tickets' message), not a 500 or blank response.
findings.finding: AC5 only seeds one audited ticket and asserts it appears; the empty-state rendering (zero tickets with audit data) is unspecified, leaving the index page's empty-path behavior untested for a new user-facing flow.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (live end-to-end test)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC5 oracle to include a second scenario: seed no audit data, start the server on an ephemeral port, GET the index, assert HTTP 200 and that the response body contains the expected empty-state marker (e.g., an empty `<ul>` or a 'No audited tickets' string).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Register `"ui"` in `rebar._optional.EXTRAS` and use `guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` (or `require_extra('ui')`) inside the CLI handler instead of a bespoke `try/except ModuleNotFoundError` + `SystemExit`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a bespoke `try/except ModuleNotFoundError` + `SystemExit` inside `server.py` for the missing-extra path, rebuilding functionality already provided by `rebar._optional.guard_import` — the codebase's canonical, tested single chokepoint for exactly this pattern (used by `agents`, `eval`, `tracing`, `grounding` extras).
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`: 'lazy-imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" SystemExit on ModuleNotFoundError'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"ui": ("fastapi", "the audit web UI (read-only local ticket browser)")` to `EXTRAS` in `src/rebar/_optional.py`, then in the `_audit` CLI handler call `guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` — which raises `OptionalDependencyError` with the standard install hint, matching every other optional extra.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `[ui]` should follow the `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` pattern (like `[llm]`/`[snapshot]`) — adding `"ui"` to `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` and resolving it via `read_reserved_section` inside `server.py` — or justify why embedding `UiConfig` directly in the core `Config` dataclass is preferable for this case.
findings.finding: The plan puts `[ui]` config directly into the core `Config` dataclass + `_SECTION_CLASSES` + `_SECTIONS`, but the established codebase pattern for optional-extra config sections is `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` + `read_reserved_section` — keeping the core `Config` dataclass free of per-extra fields (both `[llm]` and `[snapshot]` follow this pattern). The plan neither adopts this pattern nor justifies the divergence.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`: '(1) add a UiConfig dataclass … (2) add ui: UiConfig … to Config … (3) register "ui": UiConfig in _SECTION_CLASSES; (4) add "ui": {"enabled": …} to _SECTIONS'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `show` from the `_audit` dispatcher's scope in this plan; wire only the `serve` subcommand now and add `show` dispatch in the story that actually defines the `show` subcommand.
findings.finding: The `_audit` dispatcher is specified to dispatch both `serve` and `show` subcommands, but only `serve` is defined anywhere in this plan. The `show` arm is dead code at delivery — a YAGNI introduction.
findings.location: Scope — CLI section: '`_audit` dispatches `serve`/`show`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`_audit` dispatches `serve`/`show`' with '`_audit` dispatches `serve` (and will be extended to `show` in the sibling rendering story)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the rationale premise: replace 'silently dropped' with 'warned-and-dropped' (a `logger.warning` is emitted for unknown sections), so the AC oracle's 'absence of the unknown-key warning' condition remains unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '[ui] section is silently dropped as an unknown section by coerce_sparse' but the actual code at _config_schema.py:591 emits `logger.warning('rebar config%s: unknown section [%s] ignored', ...)` — the drop is warned, not silent. This is a minor inaccuracy in the rationale premise, but the conclusion (full wiring in _SECTIONS/_SECTION_CLASSES is required) still holds.
findings.location: Scope section: '(a partial edit is silently dropped as an unknown section by coerce_sparse)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'silently dropped as an unknown section by coerce_sparse' to 'warned-and-dropped (logger.warning) as an unknown section by coerce_sparse' in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'reusing the reviewbot stack' claim: the `[ui]` extra reuses `fastapi` and `uvicorn[standard]` from the reviewbot stack but adds `jinja2` as a net-new dependency (not currently in `reviewbot`); update the rationale to say 'reusing fastapi + uvicorn from the reviewbot stack, adding jinja2 for templating'.
findings.finding: The plan asserts it is 'reusing the reviewbot web stack' for the `[ui]` extra, but `jinja2` is NOT present in the `reviewbot` extra (`reviewbot = ['fastapi', 'uvicorn[standard]', 'boto3']` in pyproject.toml). The `[ui]` extra introduces `jinja2` as a net-new dependency not currently in the reviewbot stack, so the 'reusing' characterisation is partially inaccurate.
findings.location: Scope section: 'add `[project.optional-dependencies] ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]` (reusing the reviewbot stack)'; Approach rationale: 'Reuse the reviewbot FastAPI/uvicorn stack'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope and Approach rationale sections, change 'reusing the reviewbot stack' to 'reusing fastapi + uvicorn[standard] from the reviewbot extra (jinja2 is a net-new addition not currently in reviewbot)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document (or explicitly accept) that `rebar help audit` will return 'Error: unknown subcommand audit' (matching the established `workflow`/`llm` behavior), since the intercept fires only on `argv[0]=='audit'`, not on `rebar help audit` — confirm this is the intended UX.
findings.finding: The plan states the `audit` intercept mirrors `workflow`/`llm`/`explain`, but `explain` is specifically mentioned as an example that 'owns its --help like review-plan, so no help/*.txt or dispatch arm.' Reading main() confirms explain fires at line 409 (before `first = argv[0]` at line 490). However, the plan does NOT address how `rebar --help` / `rebar help audit` would behave — since audit has no `help/*.txt`, `_emit_subcommand_help('audit')` returns `None` and yields 'Error: unknown subcommand audit' to stderr. This is not a bug (it matches explain/workflow behavior) but the plan is silent on it, which may surprise operators who run `rebar help audit`.
findings.location: CLI section: 'NO `help/*.txt` file and NO `known_subcommands` change'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the rationale in the `config.py` scope bullet: replace 'this module re-exports every _config_schema class' with the accurate description that it re-exports the PUBLIC config section dataclasses (IdentityConfig is NOT currently re-exported, so the 'every class' claim is false).
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding UiConfig as a re-export in src/rebar/config.py, stating this module 're-exports every _config_schema class'. However, config.py does NOT re-export IdentityConfig (confirmed by grep: zero matches). The re-export list (lines 30-40) is already selective, not exhaustive. The plan's stated rationale ('this module re-exports every _config_schema class') is factually incorrect, though the actual instruction (add a UiConfig re-export line) is still actionable. This shouldn't block execution but could mislead an implementer into thinking they need to audit/fix other missing re-exports as part of this story.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/config.py — re-export UiConfig (this module re-exports every _config_schema class)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical with: '(this module re-exports the public config-section dataclasses; add `from rebar._config_schema import UiConfig as UiConfig` following the pattern of the other section re-exports)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `resolve_with_sources` / `rebar config` as a consumer of the `_SECTIONS` addition and explicitly confirm (or decide against) surfacing the `[ui]` section in `rebar config` output for all users regardless of extra installation.
findings.finding: The plan adds `ui` to `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `_SECTIONS` in `_config_schema.py`, making it a first-class CORE section (coerced into the stdlib Config dataclass). However, the plan's stated motivation is that ui is OPTIONAL and disabled-by-default — architecturally analogous to `llm` and `snapshot`, which are in `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` and resolved by their owning optional layer. By adding `ui` to the CORE `Config` dataclass, the `ui` field (with `UiConfig` type) becomes part of EVERY `Config` instance regardless of whether the extra is installed. This is a deliberate design choice the plan states, but it creates an unenumerated consumer impact: `resolve_with_sources` (config.py:813-839) iterates `_SECTIONS` to build its `sources` output, so `rebar config` will now display a `[ui]` section for ALL users — including those who never install the extra and don't know about it.
findings.location: Scope section: '_config_schema.py — (3) register "ui": UiConfig in _SECTION_CLASSES; (4) add "ui": {"enabled": ...} to the _SECTIONS coercer map'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/audit/__init__.py` to the scope as a new file to create (following the review_bot/__init__.py pattern of documenting the importability contract).
findings.finding: The plan adds `src/rebar/audit/` as a new package (with `src/rebar/audit/server.py`), but does not mention creating `src/rebar/audit/__init__.py`. Without an `__init__.py`, Python will treat `rebar.audit` as a namespace package, which may work but is inconsistent with every other rebar sub-package (review_bot, llm, graph, attest, etc., all have __init__.py). The plan also does not name the `_audit` dispatcher function's home module (it's a new function in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` — but the audit subpackage itself is unnamed as a new creation target).
findings.location: Scope section: 'New src/rebar/audit/server.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet: 'New `src/rebar/audit/__init__.py` — importability contract doc (mirrors `review_bot/__init__.py`; fastapi import stays in server.py only so `import rebar.audit` stays dependency-free).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `"ui": ("fastapi", "<blurb>")` to `EXTRAS` in `src/rebar/_optional.py` and use `guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` / `require_extra('ui')` in `server.py` instead of an inline `ModuleNotFoundError` catch, matching the established single-chokepoint pattern.
findings.finding: The plan routes the lazy `fastapi` import catch inline (`try: import fastapi / except ModuleNotFoundError: sys.exit(...)`) instead of extending the existing `_optional.py` `EXTRAS` registry and calling `guard_import`/`require_extra`. This bypasses the codebase's single-chokepoint pattern for optional-extra errors, produces an error message in a different format from every other extra, and leaves `extra_installed('ui')` always returning `False` for the `ui` extra (since no probe module is registered). Any future code that wants to programmatically check whether `ui` is installed — or any test using the established `require_extra`/`OptionalDependencyError` path — cannot do so.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py` (lazy import + ModuleNotFoundError handling); `_optional.py` EXTRAS dict (absent)
findings.suggested_fix: In `_optional.py` EXTRAS dict add: `"ui": ("fastapi", "the audit UI web surface (read-only local ticket browser)")`. In `server.py` and the CLI handler, call `require_extra('ui')` (raises `OptionalDependencyError` naming the install) instead of a bare `try/except ModuleNotFoundError`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or justify the `show` subcommand dispatch from the leaf's CLI scope: either delete `show` from `_audit`'s dispatch table (leaving only `serve` as the parent authorizes), or update the parent epic first to explicitly include a `show` subcommand and re-review the parent.
findings.finding: The leaf introduces a `show` subcommand (`_audit` dispatches `serve`/`show`) that has no basis in the parent epic's plan. The parent exclusively names `rebar audit serve`; no `show` subcommand appears anywhere in the parent's What, Scope, Success Criteria, or ACs.
findings.location: Scope section (CLI bullet): '`_audit` dispatches `serve`/`show`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the leaf's CLI Scope bullet, change '`_audit` dispatches `serve`/`show`' to '`_audit` dispatches `serve`'. If a `show` subcommand is genuinely desired, add it to the parent epic's plan and re-review the parent before re-reviewing this leaf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a short paragraph to 'Approach rationale' acknowledging `editor_server.py`'s stdlib `http.server` loopback pattern as in-tree prior art and explicitly justifying why FastAPI+uvicorn is preferred over it for the audit UI (e.g. async routing, Jinja2 template engine, or growth path for future endpoints).
findings.finding: The plan does not acknowledge `src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor_server.py` — an in-tree stdlib `http.server` loopback server that already serves a read-only local UI to a browser at 127.0.0.1 — which is prior art for this exact use-case (human-only, loopback-only, read-only local page) with zero new runtime dependencies. The plan dismisses 'bespoke http.server' without considering that this pattern is already proven and dependency-free in the same codebase.
findings.location: Approach rationale — 'Reuse the reviewbot FastAPI/uvicorn stack rather than adding Flask or a bespoke http.server'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach rationale, add: 'The workflow editor (`editor_server.py`) already proves stdlib `http.server` works for a loopback read UI; we choose FastAPI here because [specific reason, e.g.: async request handling, native Jinja2 templating integration, parity with the reviewbot stack for future extension] — the tradeoff is [N] new packages vs zero for the stdlib approach.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the human/admin auth contract for the non-loopback `--host` path: either (a) name a planned identity mechanism (e.g., HTTP basic auth, token header, OS-user check) that a follow-on story will implement before the flag is considered production-ready, or (b) make the flag hard-disabled until auth is implemented (i.e., ship the warning but reject non-loopback binding at startup in this story), so no unauthenticated non-loopback service can be started.
findings.finding: The plan implements a `--host` flag that allows binding to non-loopback addresses (e.g., 0.0.0.0), making the service reachable from untrusted networks, but defers any human/admin authentication for that mode entirely ('out of scope for this epic — documented, not implemented'). No named identity mechanism, authenticating-gateway requirement, or equivalent safeguard is specified for the non-loopback path.
findings.location: Endpoint access contract section; Scope > `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; AC line: '`rebar audit serve --host 0.0.0.0` prints a non-loopback exposure warning to stderr'
findings.suggested_fix: In this story, make `--host <non-loopback>` exit with an error (not just a warning): 'Non-loopback binding is not yet supported; authentication is required and not yet implemented. Track <issue-link>.' This ensures the flag API is established without shipping an unauthenticated network surface. Document the planned auth mechanism (e.g., token-in-URL or HTTP basic auth) in docs/config.md as a future story, satisfying the endpoint access contract for that mode.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `server.py`'s lazy-import guard covers both the `fastapi` absent case AND the `jinja2` absent case (FastAPI raises `RuntimeError`, not `ModuleNotFoundError`, when jinja2 is missing but fastapi is present), and document in the plan that both exceptions must be caught or that the guard imports `jinja2` explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan says it 'reuses the reviewbot web stack' but `jinja2` is not present in the `reviewbot` extra (`["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]`) and no existing FastAPI app in the codebase uses `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates`. The `[ui]` extra correctly declares `jinja2`, but `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` is only importable when `jinja2` is installed at runtime. The plan does not call out that the Jinja2Templates import must itself be lazy/guarded (inside the same `try: import fastapi... except ModuleNotFoundError` block) or that it requires jinja2 to be present at the point `fastapi.templating` is first accessed — a subtle but real import-time dependency ordering issue distinct from the fastapi guard.
findings.location: Scope / `src/rebar/audit/server.py` description; pyproject.toml `[project.optional-dependencies] ui`
findings.suggested_fix: In `server.py`'s lazy-import block, catch both `ModuleNotFoundError` and `RuntimeError` (or import `jinja2` directly alongside `fastapi` so a missing jinja2 surfaces as `ModuleNotFoundError`): `try: import fastapi; import jinja2; import uvicorn; except ModuleNotFoundError: sys.exit("install nava-rebar[ui]")`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit note to the Endpoint access contract (and `docs/config.md`) that the server speaks plain HTTP and that TLS (e.g., via a reverse proxy) is the operator's responsibility when using `--host <non-loopback>`, so the plaintext-in-transit posture is declared rather than silent.
findings.finding: The plan enables a network-reachable HTTP server (via `--host <non-loopback>`) without mentioning TLS. Audit data — ticket content and review findings — would be transmitted in plaintext over any network the user exposes the server to. The plan neither acknowledges this gap nor documents whether TLS is expected (e.g., via a reverse proxy).
findings.location: Endpoint access contract section; Scope section (server.py, uvicorn binding)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Endpoint access contract section, append: 'The server speaks plain HTTP. When `--host` is set to a non-loopback address, TLS termination (e.g., nginx/caddy reverse proxy) is the operator's responsibility. The shipped server does not handle certificates.' Mirror this warning in the `--host` CLI help string.

findings.finding: The plan states a `[ui]` section absent from `_SECTIONS` 'is silently dropped as an unknown section by `coerce_sparse`', but `coerce_sparse` actually WARNS on unknown sections (line 591: `logger.warning(…)`) and hard-errors with `strict=True`. This mischaracterises the existing behavior and may mislead implementers about what happens before the wiring is complete (e.g., during an incremental rollout where only some of the 4 edits are applied).
findings.location: Scope section: '`src/rebar/_config_schema.py` — the FULL `UiConfig` wiring (a partial edit is silently dropped as an unknown section by `coerce_sparse`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'silently dropped as an unknown section by `coerce_sparse`' with 'logged as an unknown-section WARNING by `coerce_sparse`', and note that partial wiring will produce warning noise in tests that assert clean config loading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Approach rationale' to note that the `[ui]` extra is a NEW extra (not a reuse of `[reviewbot]`) that adds `jinja2` alongside `fastapi`+`uvicorn`; clarify whether `jinja2` should also be added to `[reviewbot]` or remain `[ui]`-only.
findings.finding: The plan claims to be 'reusing the reviewbot web stack' as its primary prior-art justification, but the `reviewbot` extra (`["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]`) does NOT include `jinja2`. The plan's own Scope section correctly lists `jinja2` as a new dep in the `[ui]` extra, meaning the `[ui]` extra diverges from the reviewbot stack. The 'reusing' framing is an unverified capability assertion that conceals a real dependency addition.
findings.location: Scope — `pyproject.toml` bullet; Approach rationale — 'Reuse the reviewbot FastAPI/uvicorn stack'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit access class for the `--host <non-loopback>` path (e.g., 'public-read, no auth, safe only on trusted private networks') in the Endpoint access contract section, and document this class in the `--host` flag's help text and in `docs/config.md`, so the posture is declared rather than silently deferred.
findings.finding: The `--host <non-loopback>` path — which the plan implements and ships — has no declared access class and no auth mechanism, yet it makes the server reachable by network principals. The plan defers auth entirely ('OUT of scope for this epic — documented, not implemented') and provides only a stderr warning as the sole 'control.' Under the rubric's ambiguity-not-absence framing, the access posture of this boundary-crossing path must be explicitly declared (e.g., 'public-read, no auth, by design, lower-risk because opt-in'). An implemented flag that opens a network surface without a formal access-class declaration is an undeclared posture, not a documented deferral.
findings.location: Endpoint access contract section; AC line 4 (`--host` warning); Scope section (server.py `--host` flag)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence access-class declaration to the Endpoint access contract section: 'When `--host` is set to a non-loopback address, the surface is **public-read, unauthenticated** — safe only on a private/trusted network; the operator is responsible for any network-level access control.' Mirror this in the CLI `--help` output for `--host` and in `docs/config.md`. This converts the silent deferral into a deliberate, documented posture.

findings.finding: The plan's CLI wiring description says to list 'audit' in '_help.py::known_subcommands', but known_subcommands() is NOT a hand-maintained set — it is auto-derived by enumerating *.txt files in src/rebar/_cli/help/. A separate help/audit.txt file must be created; without it, 'rebar audit --help' returns an error path ('unknown subcommand') and 'rebar audit serve --help' is unserved, even though the intercept itself works.
findings.location: CLI section / src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands / src/rebar/_cli/help/
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt' to the Scope section, noting it is required for the file-driven known_subcommands() mechanism (parallel to how all other subcommands in _cli/help/ provide their help text).

findings.finding: The plan says the subcommand is 'listed in `_help.py::known_subcommands`', but tool-verified: `known_subcommands()` is entirely file-system driven (it enumerates `*.txt` files in `rebar/_cli/help/`) — there is NO static list to edit. Simultaneously, the plan says dispatch is registered 'mirroring the `_workflow`/`_llm` intercept', but intercepts (workflow, llm, review, etc.) are NOT in `known_subcommands()` and have NO `.txt` file. These two wiring descriptions are mutually exclusive mechanisms, and the plan gives both without resolving which applies to `audit serve`.
findings.location: CLI section: 'registered in the CLI dispatch … and listed in `_help.py::known_subcommands`'
findings.suggested_fix: Use the intercept model (matching `_workflow`): add `if argv and argv[0] == 'audit': return _audit(argv[1:])` before the `known_subcommands()` gate in `main()`, implement `_audit()` in a new `_cli/_audit_commands.py` with an argparse subparser for `serve`, and do NOT add an `audit.txt` file or touch `known_subcommands`. Remove the phrase 'listed in `_help.py::known_subcommands`' from the plan.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
